title: The Rate-Distortion-Perception Trade-Off with Algorithmic Realism

reviews.summary: This paper revisits the rate–distortion–perception (RDP) trade-off by replacing divergence-based “ensemble realism” constraints with an algorithmic-realism criterion measured by powerful critics that operate on batches of reconstructed samples. Using tools from algorithmic information theory (p-critics and universal critics), the authors define a new operational notion of perceptual realism that requires reconstructions to evade any lower semi-computable critic while allowing the critic to examine finite batches. They prove two main results: (i) when batch size grows subexponentially with blocklength, the optimal trade-off equals the classical R(1)(Δ,0) bound and is achievable with fully deterministic schemes—thus no common randomness is needed; (ii) when the batch size is extremely large (comparable to the full codebook size), their notion becomes equivalent to distribution matching (near-perfect realism), recovering prior theory where common randomness is beneficial. A one-shot achievability result (Theorem 4.2) further provides intuition via soft covering and collision bounds.
strengths: Introduces a compelling alternative to divergence-based realism by formalizing “algorithmic realism” through lower semi-computable critics acting on batches, bridging perceptual constraints and algorithmic information theory.

Provides a principled explanation for the practical observation that modern perceptual codecs do not use test-time common randomness, by showing it is unnecessary under realistic (small-batch) critics.
Connects the critic-based realism to distribution matching and proves an equivalence in the large-batch limit, neatly unifying two conceptual frameworks.

Although a theory paper, the achievability arguments are grounded in established techniques (soft-covering/channel-resolvability, birthday paradox bounds) and a careful use of universal critics from Kolmogorov complexity.

Clear conditions under which the results hold (finite alphabets, computable distributions, additive distortion, subexponential batch growth), along with a one-shot theorem that illuminates the asymptotic result.
The motivation for batch-limited critics is well argued and tied to practical perception testing (humans and common ML perceptual metrics inspect small batches).
The narrative explicitly addresses the theory–practice discrepancy regarding common randomness and explains where prior conclusions still apply (large-batch/ensemble-level constraints).
Offers a new lens to analyze realism in compression, with implications for how we design and evaluate perceptual metrics (e.g., batch size dependence).
Potentially impactful for both the information theory and machine learning communities seeking principled foundations for perceptual coding without relying on intractable common randomness.
weaknesses: Assumes finite alphabets and computable source distributions; continuous sources are only implicitly addressed via quantization/finite-precision surrogates, leaving subtle measure-theoretic issues unaddressed.

The central realism criterion is existential and untestable in practice (universal critic is not lower semi-computable), so operational calibration to real perceptual metrics (FID, LPIPS, human studies) remains indirect.
The condition R(1)(Δ,0) < H(X) in Theorem 4.1 is invoked without a fully transparent necessity discussion; it would help to clarify where the bound becomes trivial or how edge cases are treated.
No constructive or implementable coding schemes are provided beyond existence proofs; mapping the theory into practical designs remains open.
No finite-blocklength or nonasymptotic performance guarantees are derived, despite the importance of batch size in realistic deployments.
The paper is notation-heavy and occasionally difficult to follow; several definitions and remarks (e.g., on universal critics) could be streamlined with a high-level roadmap of the proof ideas.
Some artifacts from PDF extraction impede readability of equations and figures; a careful editorial pass would help.

While many key RDP references are cited, a deeper discussion contrasting the proposed “batch-critic” realism with empirical perception constraints (e.g., type/empirical realism from recent works) would help position the contribution more precisely.

More explicit connections to recent diffusion-based compression theory that operationalizes RDP with stochastic channel simulation could contextualize the “no common randomness needed” message relative to channel-synthesis approaches.
reviews.questions:
1. Could you elaborate on the necessity of the condition R(1)(Δ,0) < H(X) in Theorem 4.1? Is this simply to rule out trivial edge cases, or is it essential for the achievability/converse arguments?
2. How sensitive are your main conclusions to the choice of realism constraint in Definition 3.5 (bounded expectation of δ versus high-probability or almost-sure guarantees)? Would analogous results hold if one required sublinear growth or concentration of δ(Y) instead of a uniform bound?
3. Your framework assumes finite alphabets and computable pX. Can you outline a rigorous extension to continuous sources under quantization or Polish spaces (e.g., via uniform integrability), and whether your theorems (especially Theorem 4.1) continue to hold?
4. Do your results suggest concrete guidelines for setting batch sizes in practical evaluation (e.g., FID, LPIPS)? Is there a principled way to relate a given B to the likelihood that a critic can detect ensemble-level artifacts for typical image datasets?
5. Theorem 4.2 provides existence via random code arguments. Can you propose a constructive scheme (even if idealized) or learning-based surrogate that would plausibly approximate your codes without source knowledge? Any hints on how to design codecs that implicitly minimize a universal critic (or strong surrogates) in practice?
6. Could you comment on the role of private randomness versus determinism in your results? Theorems claim deterministic sufficiency in the small-batch regime; are there regimes where private (but not common) randomness provides a tangible advantage within your model?
7. How do your results relate to empirical perception constraints (types) in prior work (e.g., Chen et al.)? Is your batch-critic constraint strictly stronger/weaker than empirical TV constraints for finite B, and can you quantify their relationship?
reviews.assessment: This is a timely and intellectually compelling theory paper that reframes the RDP problem around algorithmic (batch-limited) critics, offering a principled resolution of a long-standing gap between theory and practice regarding the role of common randomness. The main theorems are conceptually clean and technically credible, connecting algorithmic information theory (universal critics) with soft-covering arguments to establish when deterministic schemes suffice and when common randomness matters. While the framework is abstract (finite alphabets, computability assumptions, and an untestable “universal” realism notion), the insights are practically relevant: perceptual evaluation is inherently small-batch, and thus the stringent distribution-matching requirements that mandate common randomness are often unnecessary. The lack of constructive schemes and nonasymptotic guarantees, and the heavy notation, modestly detract from accessibility, but do not diminish the paper’s core contribution. I believe this work will be of significant interest to both the information theory and ML compression communities and is suitable for publication at ICLR, provided the authors improve exposition and further clarify assumptions and implications.
